categories.label: Analog and Mixed Signal
categories.description: Circuit-level work including amplifiers, filters, converters, measurement front ends, power stages, and mixed-signal interfaces.

categories.label: Digital
categories.description: Digital design projects involving logic design, FPGA workflows, verification, timing, and ASIC-oriented architecture.

categories.label: Embedded
categories.description: MCU-based systems with firmware, board bring-up, interfaces, sensing, control, and hardware/software integration.

I also enjoy a good debate about the game, whether it’s tactics, positional awareness, or the way players carry themselves on the field.

projects.title: PWM Generating Voltage Controlled oscillator
projects.templateLabel: Clean white, blue hover, white click

templateVisual.interaction: Blue hover with a clean white click state.

projects.summary:
In many systems, timing circuits are needed not only for synchronous digital design but also to control mixed-signal circuits and interface functions.   A few examples of clocked analog circuits include analog-to-digital converters, digital-to-analog converters, switched-capacitor filters, and chopper-stabilized op-amps.  A single IC may require multiple clock frequencies for its analog and digital cells.  Since 
these clocks may not be related by simple integer ratios and created with dividers, voltage-controlled oscillators (VCO) are popular circuits to generate the required frequencies on chip.  In this project, I analyze, design, and characterize a VCO topology and use it to drive an LED via a PWM output.

The VCO was designed to produce an output frequency of approximately 1 kHz when driven by a 1 V control input. The system used a 10 V external power input, regulated down to 5 V using an LM317 voltage regulator to supply the full board.

The oscillator architecture was built around a voltage-to-current converter, current mirrors, MOS switching devices, an integrating capacitor, analog comparators, and an RS latch made from NAND gates. The control voltage sets the reference current, which charges and discharges the capacitor. As the capacitor voltage crosses the upper and lower comparator thresholds, the latch changes state and reverses the current direction, creating a continuous oscillation.

I completed the project through a full hardware design flow. This included theoretical calculations, component selection, KiCad schematic capture, LTSpice simulation, PCB layout, design-rule checking, Gerber generation, board fabrication, assembly, and bench testing. The final prototype allowed comparison between simulated and measured waveforms, subcircuit behavior, and output frequency versus control voltage. Overall, this project strengthened my understanding of analog timing circuits, current mirrors, comparator-based feedback, sequential logic, PCB design, and practical mixed-signal hardware debugging.

In a later project I am beginning to work on, a different topology of an active oscillator satisfying the Barkhausen criterion is used as the VCO in a phase-locked loop circuit.

projects.title: Smart Technology Rollator for Independence, Detection, and Empowerment (STRIDE)
projects.templateLabel: Clean white, blue hover, white click

templateVisual.interaction: Blue hover with a clean white click state.

projects.summary:
STRIDE, the Smart Technology Rollator for Independence, Detection, and Empowerment, is a senior design project developed to improve mobility, safety, and independence for users who need more support than a cane but do not require a wheelchair. Traditional rollators are useful on flat ground, but they become difficult to control on ramps and nearly impossible for many users to move safely up or down stairs. STRIDE addresses this problem by combining a powered rollator, stair-climbing mechanism, obstacle detection, embedded control, custom electronics, and an accessible user interface into one integrated prototype.

The system is built around four tri-wheel assemblies. The front tri-wheels are powered by ElectroCraft brushless DC motors through a gear-chain transmission, while the rear tri-wheels use omnidirectional wheels to preserve steering and support passive stair traversal. This allows STRIDE to move like a standard rollator on flat ground and transition into stair-climbing operation when needed. The frame was fabricated from aluminum tubing to balance strength, weight, and manufacturability, with custom motor plates, protective panels, battery mounting, and internal electronics support.

My work involved major electrical and embedded-system contributions, including motor control, battery monitoring, power distribution, firmware architecture, and system integration. STRIDE uses a centralized 24 V LiFePO₄ battery, custom power distribution board, LTC2944-based battery monitor, STM32 microcontrollers, an ESP32 wireless interface, and a Jetson Orin Nano for high-level decision-making. The STM32 handles real-time low-level control such as motor commands, brake inputs, battery readings, sensor communication, and feedback to the Jetson.

For safety, STRIDE combines a ZED 2i depth camera with ultrasonic sensors to detect obstacles, classify ground and walls, and provide redundant sensing for transparent or low-light conditions. The project demonstrates practical experience in PCB design, embedded C/C++, motor control, sensor integration, power electronics, hardware debugging, and full-system testing. STRIDE reflects my ability to turn a real user problem into a working electromechanical system.

STRIDE_CONTROL.png

sections.title: System Software
sections.description: The software driving the STRIDE system at the MCU and MPU level is still under a lot of development. ,The project started as a custom bare-metal C/C++ software environment but was migrated to a FreeRTOS model.

blocks.text: https://github.com/otienomaurice/STRIDE.git

The software driving the STRIDE system at the MCU and MPU level is still under a lot of development. ,The project started as a custom bare-metal C/C++ software environment but was migrated to a FreeRTOS model.

projects.title: STRIDE- Battery Status Monitor
projects.templateLabel: Steel teal, teal hover, ice click

templateVisual.interaction: Teal hover with a bright ice-blue click state.

projects.summary:
In many powered electric devices, batteries supply power to drive the motors and the electronics. As the devices consume the energy, the battery gets depleted over time, requiring recharging after certain thresholds are crossed. As a result, the available charge at any given moment must be tracked to alert the users to charge the batteries. The system may also shut down some functionality when the available capacity goes beyond a specified minimum. The STRIDE  (SIn many powered electric devices, batteries supply energy to both the motors and the onboard electronics. As the system operates, the battery gradually depletes and must be recharged once it reaches defined thresholds. For this reason, the available charge must be tracked continuously so users can be warned before the system reaches a low-energy state. In safety-critical powered mobility devices, battery information is even more important because low charge can reduce motor performance, interrupt control electronics, or prevent the device from completing demanding actions such as ramp movement or stair traversal. A well-designed battery status management system allows the device to alert the user, limit nonessential functions, or safely shut down before the battery reaches an unsafe operating condition.

The STRIDE system uses a 24 V LiFePO₄ battery with an internal Battery Management System. However, because this BMS is built into the commercial battery pack and does not provide a communication interface to the main controller, STRIDE required a separate battery status monitor. This custom monitor gives the system direct access to voltage, current, temperature, and accumulated charge data instead of relying only on battery terminal voltage, which is not a reliable indicator of remaining capacity for LiFePO₄ batteries.

The battery monitor PCB was designed around the LTC2944 battery gas gauge and a 1 mΩ high-current shunt resistor placed in series with the battery path. As current flows through the shunt, the LTC2944 measures the small voltage drop, calculates current, monitors battery voltage, and performs coulomb counting to estimate charge usage over time. The board also includes 3.3 V logic support for STM32-compatible I²C communication, status LEDs, fault indication, and thermal support for the shunt resistor.

On the software side, a custom STM32 driver was developed to communicate with the LTC2944 over I²C. The firmware reads measurement registers, converts the raw data into usable battery values, and updates the STRIDE system state. This allows the Jetson Nano and STM32 control architecture to use battery data for user warnings, caregiver feedback, safety decisions, and shutdown logic. By combining custom PCB hardware with embedded software, the battery monitor turns a closed commercial battery pack into an observable and system-aware energy source.

The battery monitor PCB was designed around the LTC2944 battery gas gauge and a 1 mΩ high-current shunt resistor placed in series with the battery path. As current flows through the shunt, the LTC2944 measures the small voltage drop, calculates current, monitors battery voltage, and performs coulomb counting to estimate charge usage over time. The board also includes 3.3 V logic support for STM32-compatible I²C communication, status LEDs, fault indication, and thermal support for the shunt resistor.

On the software side, a custom STM32 driver was developed to communicate with the LTC2944 over I²C. The firmware reads measurement registers, converts the raw data into usable battery values, and updates the STRIDE system state. This allows the Jetson Nano and STM32 control architecture to use battery data for user warnings, caregiver feedback, safety decisions, and shutdown logic. By combining custom PCB hardware with embedded software, the battery monitor turns a closed commercial battery pack into an observable and system-aware energy source.

items.title: STRIDE Battery Status Monitor Board User Manual.pdf

projects.title: STRIDE - The Motor Control System
projects.templateLabel: Graphite ASIC, slate hover, white click

templateVisual.interaction: Slate hover with a crisp white click state.

projects.summary:
The STRIDE motor system was designed to provide reliable powered mobility on flat ground while also delivering enough torque for stair traversal. The architecture uses two ElectroCraft MP24 brushless DC motors, one on each side of the rollator, to drive the front tri-wheel assemblies. Each motor includes an integrated right-angle gearbox with a 21:1 reduction ratio, providing high torque at the low speeds needed for a mobility device. The motors are powered from the 24 V LiFePO₄ battery through the tractive power path, while the ElectroCraft EZ Drive controllers handle the high-power three-phase commutation required by the BLDC motors.

The motor control system is organized as a layered architecture. The Jetson Orin Nano acts as the high-level decision-making unit, interpreting user commands, obstacle detection results, mode selection, and system safety states. It sends motion commands to the STM32 system microcontroller, which performs the low-level real-time control. This separation keeps safety-critical motor signaling on the STM32 while allowing the Jetson to focus on higher-level system behavior.

Figure: The Motor amplifier PCB

The STM32 does not directly drive the motor phases. Instead, it sends command signals to the EZ Drive motor controllers. Motor speed is controlled using the STM32’s DAC output, which produces a 0–3.3 V analog signal. Since the EZ Drive requires a 0–10 V speed command, I developed a custom motor control interface board using a non-inverting op-amp amplifier with a gain of approximately 3. This scales the MCU output to the full command range needed for the motor controller.

In addition to analog speed control, the STM32 manages digital signals for enable, braking, and direction. It also reads feedback such as fault status and speed-related signals from the motor controller. Testing confirmed that the amplifier output was highly linear, with a measured gain of about 3.02, allowing predictable mapping between MCU command voltage and motor speed. Overall, this architecture combines high-power BLDC actuation, safe low-level embedded control, and modular system-level decision-making.

subsections.description: I enjoy creating projects from the ground up as well as reverse engineering existing ones, taking them from the initial idea to full realization. This often involves working on everything from project constraints and budget to design, implementation, testing, and validation.
